example:
Come on, spill the beans!
She spilled the beans about the party.
Don't spill the beans yet.

It's late; let's hit the road.
We hit the road early.
Time to hit the road.

meaning: finally decide to do something risky
example:
They took the plunge and got married.
I finally took the plunge.
She took the plunge and quit.

term: the early bird catches the worm
meaning: those who act first get the advantage
example:
Get there early; the early bird catches the worm.
The early bird catches the worm, so apply now.
Remember, the early bird catches the worm.

meaning: what you do matters more than what you say
example:
Actions speak louder than words.
Show me; actions speak louder than words.
Remember, actions speak louder than words.

meaning: doing something late is better than not at all
example:
You finished it; better late than never.
Better late than never, I suppose.
He apologised at last; better late than never.

meaning: other situations seem better than your own
example:
He envies them, but the grass is always greener.
The grass is always greener on the other side.
Don't move; the grass is always greener.

meaning: very common and of little value
example:
Such apps are a dime a dozen.
Cheap copies are a dime a dozen.
Ideas are a dime a dozen.

meaning: simple to suggest but hard to do
example:
Saving money is easier said than done.
That's easier said than done.
Quitting is easier said than done.

The party got out of hand.
Costs are getting out of hand.
Don't let it get out of hand.

term: in the heat of the moment
example:
He said it in the heat of the moment.
In the heat of the moment, I shouted.
Don't decide in the heat of the moment.

Keep your chin up; things will improve.
She kept her chin up.
Chin up, you'll be fine.

It's a free trial, no strings attached.
He offered help with no strings attached.
The gift came with no strings attached.

You're on thin ice with the boss.
He's skating on thin ice.
After that, I was on thin ice.

A refund is out of the question.
Quitting now is out of the question.
That's completely out of the question.

If you blame me, you're barking up the wrong tree.
The police were barking up the wrong tree.
You're barking up the wrong tree there.

She's famous but very down to earth.
He's a down-to-earth person.
I like how down to earth they are.

That car costs the earth.
It cost the earth to repair.
A good one doesn't have to cost the earth.

I can't wrap my head around the rules.
It takes time to wrap your head around it.
She wrapped her head around the maths.

He'll tidy his room when pigs fly.
Sure, when pigs fly.
That'll happen when pigs fly.
